summary:
The Battle of Talikota (January 23, 1565 AD) was the decisive confrontation between the Vijayanagara Empire — the last great Hindu empire of medieval India — and the allied [[event:2487|Deccan Sultanates]] of Bijapur, Ahmednagar, Bidar, and Golconda, resulting in a catastrophic Vijayanagara defeat and the subsequent sack of Hampi (the Vijayanagara capital) that effectively ended the empire as a major power. The Vijayanagara Empire, which had dominated southern India for over two centuries, was the primary Hindu cultural and political counterweight to the Deccan's Muslim sultans; its defeat removed the last significant obstacle to Muslim political supremacy in the Deccan and opened the south to eventual Mughal penetration.

The battle was decided by the presence of Muslim artillery and the defection of two commanders within the Vijayanagara army. The Vijayanagara king Aliya Rama Raya, who had personally commanded and outmaneuvered the Deccan sultans for decades, was captured during the fighting and immediately beheaded; his death caused his army to dissolve in panic. The subsequent sack of Hampi was thorough and prolonged — the rich capital, known as one of the world's greatest cities, was burned, looted, and systematically destroyed over several months. The ruins at Hampi (now a UNESCO World Heritage Site) remain among the most evocative in India — vast stone temples, market streets, and palace foundations that testify to the civilization's wealth. Vijayanagara's collapse opened southern India to a complex century of political fragmentation before [[event:2507|Aurangzeb]] brought Mughal armies deep into the Deccan.
